games.explanation: Sonny Gray's surface numbers look fine until you notice righties have actually hit him hard this year, and Minnesota happens to roll out a lineup full of them. Buxton and Bell are the exact bat profiles that punish a command-and-breaking-ball righty, and the wind blowing in at Fenway turns this from a slugfest into the kind of grinding four-run game where the better lineup wins by an inch. The sim has it a coin flip; the board has it closer to a 42 percent dog. At +136 you're getting paid like the underdog when the matchup says pick'em.

The Yankees are the name brand here, but Tampa Bay is the better team on paper and the better team on the mound, and the board is selling them like a coin flip. Rasmussen is the cleaner arm by a wide margin, and Ryan Weathers has spent the year getting barreled up by righties (a 9.6% barrel rate against tells you everything you need about Aranda and Caminero's evening). Judge will do Judge things, but the rest of that lineup is thinner than the price suggests, and a wind blowing in at 11 mph quietly turns a few would-be Yankee Stadium souvenirs into outs. Getting plus money on the side with the pitching edge is the entire argument.

The market keeps treating the Marlins like a doormat in this matchup, and the Mets keep reminding everyone they are a coin-flip team wearing a contender's jersey. Miami's lineup against righties is quietly functional, with Stowers and Lopez carrying the kind of contact profiles that punish a guy like Phillips into mistakes, and Juan Soto can only bat so many times in a closed-roof game that projects inside a single run. Phillips is nobody's ace, but a 17.4% strikeout rate plays fine when the goal is just keeping the game close enough for the bullpen to flip a coin. Getting plus money on a team your math makes a 57% favorite is the entire bet, and the Mets being the Mets is the kicker.

Reid Detmers gets credit for the strikeouts and almost no scrutiny for the contact he gives up when hitters don't chase, and Texas is built to make him work. The Rangers stack right-handed bats that have been squaring him up all year, and his barrel rate against righties sits above 11%, which is the kind of number that turns a fly-ball night into a problem at a park playing slightly homer-friendly. Gore is the steadier arm in this matchup even if the ERA column doesn't say so, and the wind blowing in actually helps him more than it hurts the Texas lineup against a flyball lefty. Getting plus money on the better team in a coin-flip game is the entire reason to be here.

picks.explanation: Sonny Gray's surface numbers look fine until you notice righties have actually hit him hard this year, and Minnesota happens to roll out a lineup full of them. The market keeps treating the Marlins like a doormat in this matchup, and the Mets keep reminding everyone they are a coin-flip team wearing a contender's jersey. Miami's lineup against righties is quietly functional, with Stowers and Lopez carrying the kind of contact profiles that punish a guy like Phillips into mistakes, and Juan Soto can only bat so many times in a closed-roof game that projects inside a single run. Phillips is nobody's ace, but a 17.4% strikeout rate plays fine when the goal is just keeping the game close enough for the bullpen to flip a coin. Getting plus money on a team your math makes a 57% favorite is the entire bet, and the Mets being the Mets is the kicker.

The board is pricing this like the Padres are going to win comfortably, but Michael King is the exact wrong arm to bury a lineup with Nick Kurtz and Brent Rooker waiting in it. King gives up hard contact at a barrel rate north of 11% and walks his share, which keeps innings alive against a top of the order that punishes right-handers as well as anyone the A's can run out there. Kurtz in particular has posted a .423 xwOBA against righties this year, the kind of damage profile that turns a projected one-run game into a coinflip on the spread. San Diego is the better team, sure, but "better team" and "wins by two" are different bets, and the sim has this finishing inside a run. At -145 you're getting the runs against a starter built to leak them.

Reid Detmers gets credit for the strikeouts and almost no scrutiny for the contact he gives up when hitters don't chase, and Texas is built to make him work. The Rangers stack right-handed bats that have been squaring him up all year, and his barrel rate against righties sits above 11%, which is the kind of number that turns a fly-ball night into a problem at a park playing slightly homer-friendly. Gore is the steadier arm in this matchup even if the ERA column doesn't say so, and the wind blowing in actually helps him more than it hurts the Texas lineup against a flyball lefty. Getting plus money on the better team in a coin-flip game is the entire reason to be here.
